sections.content:

You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player didn't seem to move - the white cross is still at the same position. Let me check more carefully by comparing the before and after frames. The board_changed is True but the player area looks the same. Maybe only the timer/progress bar changed?
Let me check what actually changed by comparing previous_frame to current_frame, and also look at the bottom bar area.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed - compare previous to current
# First check if player actually moved by looking at segmentation
prev_seg = previous_frame.segmentation
curr_seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Find white (W) node in both frames
def find_node_by_color(seg, color):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == color:
            return n
    return None

prev_w = find_node_by_color(prev_seg, 'W')
curr_w = find_node_by_color(curr_seg, 'W')
print("Prev W boundary:", prev_w['boundary'] if prev_w else "None")
print("Curr W boundary:", curr_w['boundary'] if curr_w else "None")

# Check the bottom bar for changes (timer)
lines_prev = previous_frame.ascii.split('\n')
lines_curr = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nBottom bar prev (row 61):", lines_prev[61][10:65])
print("Bottom bar curr (row 61):", lines_curr[61][10:65])

# Check if yellow bar changed length
for r in [61, 62]:
    print(f"Row {r} prev: {lines_prev[r][10:65]}")
    print(f"Row {r} curr: {lines_curr[r][10:65]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player (W node) didn't move - same boundary. The only change is in the bottom bar (timer/progress bar) - one Y changed to G (gray). This is a HUD/timer element that changes with each step.
So RIGHT didn't move the player. The player is blocked from moving right by the dark charcoal obstacle. Let me try moving in other directions - UP, DOWN, or LEFT to see which directions work.
Let me try moving UP since there seems to be open space above the player in the gray area.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player didn't move RIGHT - blocked by obstacle. Try UP
result = action(['UP'])
print("Action result:", result)

# Check player position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        print("Player W boundary:", n['boundary'])
        break

# Check player area
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Player area (rows 28-35, cols 18-26):")
for r in range(28, 36):
    print(f"  {r}: {lines[r][18:26]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
